section: THE BEHAVIOR OF A NEUTRAL LOCUS LINKED TO A SELECTED SITE
subsection: Allele-frequency Change
nearby_text: introduced into the population as a single copy and is destined to become fixed, its approximate time to fixation is $ \tau \simeq 2 \ln(4N_{e}s)/s $. Substituting this time into Equation 8.2a yields $$ \delta_{q}(\infty)=\delta_{q}(0)e^{-c\tau}\simeq\delta_{q}(0)\exp\left[-(2c/s)\ln(4N_{e}s)\right]=\delta_{q}(0)\left(4N_{e}s\right)^{-2c/s} $$ (8.2b) so that $$ \frac{\delta_{q}(\infty)}{\delta_{q}(0)}=\left(\frac{1}{4N_{e}s}\right)^{2c/s} $$ (8.2c)

label: Formula 8.4d
section: Hitchhiking and Selective Sweeps: Introduction
subsection: Allele-frequency Change
nearby_text: equency (essentially becoming fixed), given it started as a single copy, is given by replacing $ \epsilon = 1/(2N) $ by $$ \epsilon=\frac{1}{2N}\frac{N}{2sN_{e}}=\frac{1}{4N_{e}s} $$ which results in $$ \tau=2\ln(4N_{e}s)/s $$ (8.4d)

label: Formula 8.4e
section: Hitchhiking and Selective Sweeps: Introduction
subsection: Allele-frequency Change
nearby_text: 2012). Again, starting at an initial frequency of $ \epsilon \simeq 0 $, Equation 8.4a yields $$ \frac{x}{1-x}=\epsilon e^{st_{x}} $$ which, upon rearranging (with $ \epsilon = 1/[4N_{e}s] $), yields $$ t_{x}=\frac{1}{s}\ln\left(\frac{\epsilon^{-1}x}{1-x}\right)=\frac{1}{s}\ln\left(\frac{4N_{e}s x}{1-x}\right) $$ (8.4e)

label: Formula 8.5a
section: Hitchhiking and Selective Sweeps: Introduction
subsection: Allele-frequency Change
nearby_text: $c/s < 0.1$, the integral is close to one, and we recover Equation 8.3a, $\Delta_q = \delta_q(0) p(0)^{c/s}$. Barton (Otto and Barton 1997; Barton 1998) showed that a more accurate result is given by $$ \Delta_{q}\simeq\delta_{q}(0)p(0)^{c/s}\{\Gamma\left[1+(c/s)\right]\}^{2}\Gamma\left[1-(c/s)\right] $$ (8.5a) where $ \Gamma $ denotes the gamma function (Equation 2.25b). For $ c/s < 0.1 $, this reduces to $$ \Delta_{q}\simeq\delta_{q}(0)\left(1+\frac{c}{s}\left[\ln[p(0)]+0.5772\right]\right) $$ (8.5b) whic

label: Formula 8.7a
section: THE BEHAVIOR OF A NEUTRAL LOCUS LINKED TO A SELECTED SITE
subsection: Reduction in Genetic Diversity Around a Sweep
nearby_text: ity, $ \pi $ (Chapters 2 and 4). Hitchhiking during the fixation of a linked selected allele changes a marker allele frequency to $ q(\infty) = q(0) + \Delta_q $, and hence the heterozygosity becomes $$ \begin{aligned}H&=2q(\infty)[1-q(\infty)]=2[q(0)+\Delta_{q}][1-(q(0)+\Delta_{q})]\\&=H_{0}-2[1-2q(0)]\Delta_{q}-2\left(\Delta_{q}\right)^{2}\end{aligned} $$ (8.7a)

  "nearby_text": "ity, $ \\pi $ (Chapters 2 and 4). Hitchhiking during the fixation of a linked selected allele changes a marker allele frequency to $ q(\\infty) = q(0) + \\Delta_q $, and hence the heterozygosity becomes $$ \\begin{aligned}H&=2q(\\infty)[1-q(\\infty)]=2[q(0)+\\Delta_{q}][1-(q(0)+\\Delta_{q})]\\\\&=H_{0}-2[1-2q(0)]\\Delta_{q}-2\\left(\\Delta_{q}\\right)^{2}\\end{aligned} $$ (8.7a)"
}

label: Formula 8.7b
section: THE BEHAVIOR OF A NEUTRAL LOCUS LINKED TO A SELECTED SITE
subsection: Reduction in Genetic Diversity Around a Sweep
nearby_text: ises on a non- $ m $ background, yielding $ q_A(0) = 0 $, $ \delta_q(0) = 0 - q(0) = -q(0) $ and $ \Delta_q \simeq -q(0) $ $ p(0)^{c/s} $. Using these results, the expected allele-frequency change is $$ \mathrm{E}(\Delta_{q})=q(0)\cdot\left[1-q(0)\right]p(0)^{c/s}+\left[1-q(0)\right]\cdot\left[-q(0)p(0)^{c/s}\right]=0 $$ (8.7b)

label: Formula 8.8b
section: THE BEHAVIOR OF A NEUTRAL LOCUS LINKED TO A SELECTED SITE
subsection: Reduction in Genetic Diversity Around a Sweep
nearby_text: Recalling that this result is an approximation (as Equation 8.3a approximates the allele frequency change), our final result is $$ \frac{H_{h}}{H_{0}}\simeq1-p(0)^{2c/s}\simeq-\frac{2c}{s}\ln[p(0)]\quad for\quad c/s\ll1 $$ (8.8b)

section: Hitchhiking and Selective Sweeps: Introduction

nearby_text: of $ 1 - f_s^2 $, two randomly drawn alleles are not identical-by-descent (from the sweep), displaying the pre-sweep heterozygosity value. Hence, from Equation 8.3b, which assumes additive selection, $$ \frac{H_{h}}{H_{0}}=1-f_{s}^{2}=1-p(0)^{2c/s} $$ (8.8d)

nearby_text: this critical value. Assuming there is a single copy of the selected allele at the start of selection, $ p(0) = 1/(2N) $, the simple approximation given by Equation 8.8b (with $ c/s = 0.01 $) yields $$ \frac{H_{h}}{H_{0}}=\frac{2c}{s}\ln(2N)=0.02\ln(2N) $$ (8.9)

label: Formula 8.12c
section: THE BEHAVIOR OF A NEUTRAL LOCUS LINKED TO A SELECTED SITE
subsection: Effects of Sweeps on the Variance in Microsatellite Copy Number
nearby_text: Using results from Slatkin (1995b), the rate of recovery in V following the sweep is a modification of Equation 8.11 $$ \begin{align*}V(t)=V_0\left(1-p(0)^{(4\mu+2c)/s}\cdot e^{-t/(2N_e)}\right)\end{align*} $$ (8.12c) As with Equation 8.11, $ t_{0.5} \simeq 1.4N_e $ generations are required to recover half the decrease in V immediately following the sweep. It is often stated that microsatellites recover variation

label: Formula 8.13
section: THE BEHAVIOR OF A NEUTRAL LOCUS LINKED TO A SELECTED SITE
subsection: The Site-Frequency Spectrum
nearby_text: rms a presweep Watterson distribution, $ \phi(x) $, into a new post-sweep SFS, $ \phi^{*}(y) $, which is a function of the critical parameter, $ f_s $, for the impact of a sweep (Equation 8.1d), with $$ \phi^{*}(y)=\left\{\begin{aligned}&\theta\left(\frac{1}{y}-\frac{1}{1-f_{s}}\right)&\frac{1}{2N}&\leq y\leq1-f_{s}\\ &0&1-f_{s}&<y<f_{s}\\ &\frac{\theta}{1-f_{s}}&f_{s}&\leq y\leq1-\frac{1}{2N}\end{aligned}\right. $$ (8.13)

label: Formula 8.15
section: THE BEHAVIOR OF A NEUTRAL LOCUS LINKED TO A SELECTED SITE
subsection: Partial Sweeps
nearby_text: Coop and Ralph found that the reduction in heterozygosity (for a neutral marker at recombination distance c) when the sweep pauses at frequency x is $$ \frac{H_{h}}{H_{0}}\simeq1-x^{2}e^{-2c t_{x}}=1-x^{2}\left(\frac{4N_{e}s x}{1-x}\right)^{-2c/s} $$ (8.15)

label: Formula 8.26
section: THE BEHAVIOR OF A NEUTRAL LOCUS LINKED TO A SELECTED SITE
subsection: Partial Sweeps
nearby_text: impact of a single partial sweep on $ \pi $. Coop and Ralph (2012) showed that the reduction in nucleotide diversity due to recurrent partial sweeps has a form very similar to Equation 8.24b, namely $$ \frac{\pi}{\pi_{0}}\simeq\frac{1}{1+\lambda\zeta/c_{0}} $$ (8.26) where $ \zeta $ is a function of the time for the sweep to reach its target frequency, $ x $ (Equation 8.4e).

label: Formula 8.16c
section: SOFT SWEEPS
subsection: Signatures of a Soft Sweep
nearby_text: Garud et al. (2015) suggested a modified haplotype homozygosity statistic, $ H_{12} $, which lumps the first two haplotypes into a single class $$ H_{12}=(p_{1}+p_{2})^{2}+\sum_{i>3}p_{i}^{2}=\sum_{i}p_{i}^{2}+2p_{1}p_{2}=H_{1}+2p_{1}p_{2} $$ (8.16c)

label: Formula 8.17a
section: SOFT SWEEPS
subsection: How Likely is a Sweep Using Standing Variation?
nearby_text: to-be favored allele A, and $ u(x) $ its probability of fixation under the new environment given x. The probability, $ \Pr_{sv} $, that a sweep occurs using standing variation at this locus is simply $$ \Pr_{sv}=E\left[u(x)\right]=\int_{1/(2N)}^{1-1/(2N)}u(x)\phi(x)dx $$ (8.17a)

  "nearby_text": "to-be favored allele A, and $ u(x) $ its probability of fixation under the new environment given x. The probability, $ \\Pr_{sv} $, that a sweep occurs using standing variation at this locus is simply $$ \\Pr_{sv}=E\\left[u(x)\\right]=\\int_{1/(2N)}^{1-1/(2N)}u(x)\\phi(x)dx $$ (8.17a)"

label: Formula 8.17b
section: SOFT SWEEPS
subsection: How Likely is a Sweep Using Standing Variation?
nearby_text: (Chapter 7; Appendix 1). Likewise, the fixation probability under the new fitnesses can also be obtained using diffusion results (Chapter 7). Putting these together, Hermisson and Pennings found that $$ \Pr_{sv}\approx1-e^{-\theta_{b}}\ln(1+R),\quad where\quad R=\frac{2h\alpha_{b}}{2h_{d}\alpha_{d}+1} $$ (8.17b) with $ \alpha_b = 4N_e s $ and $ \alpha_d = 4N_e s_d $ being the scaled strengths of selection in the new and old environments, respectively, and $ \theta_b = 4N_e \mu_b $ being the scaled mutation r

label: Formula 8.19
section: SOFT SWEEPS
subsection: How Likely is a Sweep Using Standing Variation?
nearby_text: The total waiting time (in generations) until the fixation of a favorable allele with additive effects on fitness (h = 0.5) is approximately $$ t_{fix}=\frac{1}{s}\left(\frac{1}{\theta_{b}}+2\ln(4N_{e}s)\right) $$ (8.19) where the first term is the mean waiting time for the first appearance of a successful mutation (the establishment time; Equation 8.18b) and the second is its fixation time (Equation 8.4d). label: Formula 8.20
section: SOFT SWEEPS
subsection: How Likely is a Sweep Using Standing Variation?
nearby_text: ning on a sweep by time T (assuming that the fixation time of a successful mutation is very small relative to T), giving the probability that it is from an existing allele (i.e., a standing sweep) as $$ \begin{aligned}\Pr_{ex}(T)&=\Pr(existing allele|sweep by generation T)\\&=\frac{\Pr_{sv}}{\Pr_{sv}+(1-\Pr_{sv})\Pr_{new}(T)}\\&=\frac{1-\exp\left[-\theta_{b}\ln(1+R)\right]}{1-\exp\left\{-\theta_{b}[\ln(1+R)+Th\alpha_{b}]\right\}}\end{aligned} $$ (8.20)

label: Formula 8.22a
section: SOFT SWEEPS
subsection: Recurrent Mutation of the Favorable Allele Cannot be Ignored
nearby_text: ate the expected number of new favorable mutations that will arise by noting that the average frequency of a favored additive allele over its sojourn from near zero to near fixation is $ 1/2 $, hence $$ \begin{aligned}E(number of new favorable mutations that arise)&\approx2N(1/2)\mu_{b}\tau=(N/N_{e})(N_{e}\mu_{b})\tau\\&=(N/N_{e})(\theta_{b}/4)\cdot2\ln(4N_{e}s)/s\\&=2N\theta_{b}\ln(\alpha_{b})/\alpha_{b}\end{aligned} $$ (8.22a) as obtained by Pennings and Hermisson (2006a). Although this is the total number of recurrent favorable mutations that arise, each has only a probability of $ 2s(N_e/N) $ of increasing (Chapter 7). H

  "nearby_text": "ate the expected number of new favorable mutations that will arise by noting that the average frequency of a favored additive allele over its sojourn from near zero to near fixation is $ 1/2 $, hence $$ \\begin{aligned}E(number of new favorable mutations that arise)&\\approx2N(1/2)\\mu_{b}\\tau=(N/N_{e})(N_{e}\\mu_{b})\\tau\\\\&=(N/N_{e})(\\theta_{b}/4)\\cdot2\\ln(4N_{e}s)/s\\\\&=2N\\theta_{b}\\ln(\\alpha_{b})/\\alpha_{b}\\end{aligned} $$ (8.22a) as obtained by Pennings and Hermisson (2006a). Although this is the total number of recurrent favorable mutations that arise, each has only a probability of $ 2s(N_e/N) $ of increasing (Chapter 7). H"
}

  "nearby_text": "Wiehe and Stephan found that with recurrent sweeps, the expected heterozygosity, as measured by nucleotide diversity, $ \\pi $, at linked neutral sites, is $$ \\frac{\\pi}{\\pi_{0}}\\simeq\\frac{c_{0}}{c_{0}+\\lambda\\gamma\\kappa}=\\frac{1}{1+\\lambda\\gamma\\kappa/c_{0}} $$ (8.24b) where $ \\pi_0 = 4N_e\\mu $ is the average heterozygosity at a single site for an equilibrium neutral population under no sweeps, $ c_0 $ is the average per-nucleotide recombination rate over the regio"
}

label: Formula 8.24c
section: GENOME-WIDE IMPACT OF REPEATED SELECTION AT LINKED SITES
subsection: Impact of Recurrent Hard Sweeps on Levels of Neutral Variation
nearby_text: region of interest, and there is a constant, $ \kappa \simeq 0.075 $. For $ c_0 > \lambda \gamma\kappa $, because $ 1/(1+x) \simeq 1-x \simeq e^{-x} $ for $ |x| \ll 1 $, Equation 8.24b rearranges to $$ \frac{\pi}{\pi_{0}}\simeq1-\frac{\lambda\gamma\kappa}{c_{0}}\simeq\exp\left(-\lambda\gamma\kappa/c_{0}\right) $$ (8.24c)

For $ c_0 > \\lambda \\gamma\\kappa $, because $ 1/(1+x) \\simeq 1-x \\simeq e^{-x} $ for $ |x| \\ll 1 $, Equation 8.24b rearranges to $$ \\frac{\\pi}{\\pi_{0}}\\simeq1-\\frac{\\lambda\\gamma\\kappa}{c_{0}}\\simeq\\exp\\left(-\\lambda\\gamma\\kappa/c_{0}\\right) $$ (8.24c)"

label: Formula 8.24d
section: GENOME-WIDE IMPACT OF REPEATED SELECTION AT LINKED SITES
subsection: Impact of Recurrent Hard Sweeps on Levels of Neutral Variation
nearby_text: e linear approximation gives the Stephan regression (Stephan 1995) for explaining the average variation, $ \pi $, within a region as a function of the region's per-base recombination rate, $ c_{0} $, $$ \pi=\pi_{0}-\left(\pi_{0}\lambda\gamma\kappa\right)x,\quad\mathrm{w h e r e}x=1/c_{0} $$ (8.24d)

  "nearby_text": "e linear approximation gives the Stephan regression (Stephan 1995) for explaining the average variation, $ \\pi $, within a region as a function of the region's per-base recombination rate, $ c_{0} $, $$ \\pi=\\pi_{0}-\\left(\\pi_{0}\\lambda\\gamma\\kappa\\right)x,\\quad\\mathrm{w h e r e}x=1/c_{0} $$ (8.24d)"
}

label: Formula 8.25
section: GENOME-WIDE IMPACT OF REPEATED SELECTION AT LINKED SITES
subsection: A Few Large or Many Small Sweeps?
nearby_text: Letting $ d_a $ denote the per-site rate of amino acid divergence, and substituting $ \lambda = d_a \alpha / (2t) $ into Equation 8.24b, yields the Andolfatto regression $$ \pi\simeq\pi_{0}\frac{c}{c+\lambda\gamma\kappa}=\pi_{0}\frac{c}{c+\left[\alpha\gamma\kappa/(2t)\right]d_{a}}=\frac{\pi_{0}}{1+\beta x} $$ (8.25) where $ x = d_a / c $ and $ \beta = \alpha \gamma \kappa / (2t) $, and because $ \lambda $ is now measured on a per-amino acid basis, $ c $ is now the recombination rate per codon (Andolfatto 2007).

  "nearby_text": "Letting $ d_a $ denote the per-site rate of amino acid divergence, and substituting $ \\lambda = d_a \\alpha / (2t) $ into Equation 8.24b, yields the Andolfatto regression $$ \\pi\\simeq\\pi_{0}\\frac{c}{c+\\lambda\\gamma\\kappa}=\\pi_{0}\\frac{c}{c+\\left[\\alpha\\gamma\\kappa/(2t)\\right]d_{a}}=\\frac{\\pi_{0}}{1+\\beta x} $$ (8.25) where $ x = d_a / c $ and $ \\beta = \\alpha \\gamma \\kappa / (2t) $, and because $ \\lambda $ is now measured on a per-amino acid basis, $ c $ is now the recombination rate per codon (Andolfatto 2007)."
}

label: Formula 8.27a
section: GENOME-WIDE IMPACT OF REPEATED SELECTION AT LINKED SITES
subsection: Selective Interference and the Hill-Robertson Effect
nearby_text: $ C = Lc_0 $, where $ c_0 $ is the average recombination rate between adjacent nucleotides. If $ \Lambda_0 = L\lambda_0 $ is the expected rate of adaptation ignoring the effects of interference, then $$ \Lambda\simeq\frac{\Lambda_{0}}{1+2\Lambda_{0}/C}\quad or\quad\lambda\simeq\frac{\lambda_{0}}{1+2\lambda_{0}/c_{0}} $$ (8.27a)
